goal: Turn Templeton into a capital-allocation workspace where research, notes, watchlists, portfolio context, and monitoring live together.

product_position.primary_job_to_be_done: Help the user decide where new money should go, how much conviction exists, and what still needs to be learned before buying.
secondary_jobs: Preserve high-quality research context across time
Organize ideas by industry, theme, or portfolio role
Make thesis changes and missing work visible
Keep a decision journal rather than scattered notes
anti_goals: Do not reduce the product to one-off ticker answers with no durable organization.
Do not treat the user's own notes as separate from the research record.
Do not let watchlists become flat ticker lists with no ranking, rationale, or next steps.

core_objects.description: A durable collection of tickers grouped by industry, theme, strategy, or decision stage.
A durable record of current holdings, target exposure, overlap, and room for new capital.
A long-lived per-company record combining generated research and user-authored thinking.
A timestamped user note linked to a ticker, watchlist, portfolio, or theme.
A trigger, event, or condition that should be re-checked before or after capital moves.
A ranked list of ideas competing for new money.

examples: add ASML to my semis watchlist
remove AMD from my cyclical names list
expected_behavior: Create missing watchlists when the user clearly requests it.
Store the reason a ticker was added whenever possible.
Show ranked or grouped watchlist views, not just flat ticker dumps.

examples: what industries am I underweight in
where should new money go in my portfolio
am I too concentrated in semis
expected_behavior: Use current portfolio buckets and user preferences when available.
Separate portfolio-capacity questions from pure single-stock analysis.
Be explicit when missing holdings or sizing data limits the answer.

examples: rank my semis watchlist by readiness
which names are closest to buy-ready
what is blocking conviction on these names
expected_behavior: Rank candidates by readiness, conviction, valuation, and open-question burden.
Show why a name is high or low priority.
Surface what work remains before capital should be deployed.

examples: save a note that I only want to buy ASML on a pullback
log that my conviction in NVDA increased after earnings
record that I need to study customer concentration before buying
expected_behavior: Store user-authored notes as durable first-class records.
Link notes to tickers, watchlists, or portfolios.
Allow notes to influence future what-changed and readiness views.

examples: what should I monitor before buying more into semis
show unresolved risks across my watchlist
which held names need a fresh review
expected_behavior: Aggregate monitoring items by ticker, watchlist, and portfolio.

helpful_ideas.description: A simple status like incomplete, building, or ready that reflects whether enough work exists to commit capital.
A dedicated place to record reasons not to buy so the notebook does not become one-sided.
Industry or theme exposure targets so Templeton can answer where incremental dollars should go.
A ranked list of unresolved research questions that still block conviction.
A record of why the user bought, passed, or waited, useful for later review and learning.
User-defined conditions like valuation pullbacks, risk resolution, or event triggers required before buying.

phase_1: user note capture for a ticker
phase_2: watchlist ranking by readiness and conviction

acceptance_criteria: The user can organize names by theme or industry without leaving Templeton.
The user can store notes and open questions tied to a ticker or watchlist.
Templeton can explain what is blocking confidence before capital is committed.
The system can show where new money should go using both research quality and portfolio context.
